real_insight: When an engineer cites first-principles arguments while grieving a deprecated implementation, the contradiction is the diagnosis — they have confused implementation-loss with spec-loss; locate the actual invariant (the customer requirement) and the grief resolves.
could_act_on_tomorrow: Yes — the next time I write an unusually long deprecation PR description that eulogizes the old service, I should ask whether what I'm mourning is the spec (in which case I should be defending it in review, not deprecating) or the implementation (in which case the grief is misplaced and the PR description is writing me a story I don't need).
is_new_or_sharply_named: The named pattern — first-principles vocabulary used while reasoning at implementation depth, recognizable as a contradiction — is not a fortune-cookie observation. It names a real failure mode I have seen in senior engineers, including myself: writing about service evolution in elevated abstract terms while the actual emotional reasoning is grief over a specific schema. The verse's framing ('grief paired with wisdom-talk is a tell') is an actual diagnostic.
objection: none — the analog avoids 'embrace change' / 'be detached from outcomes' wisdom-theater. It explicitly preserves the legitimacy of grief in valid cases (institutional knowledge loss, premature deprecation). The counter-example carves out the wrong-deprecation case correctly. The implication is operational: locate the customer requirement, ask whether what you're grieving is preserved by the rewrite.
verdict_reason: Specific failure mode, specific corrective, specific test (can you name the customer requirement the deprecated module was implementing?). This passes the senior-engineer-who-never-heard-of-this-verse test.
